title: 2025/10/08 - GA Subcommittee A (1 of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-10-08 - Governance and Administration Committee - Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill [Subcommittee A]
description: Wednesday, 8th October 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: Mount Manganui ratepayers residents and retailers Tim Maltby OraTaiao Aotearoa NZ Climate and Health Counci Recreation Aotearoa Sarah Murray Wellington Community Justice Project Emily Grown Queenstown Lakes District Council Glyn Lewers, Mayor SociaLink Tuhono Papori Liz Davies Opotiki District Council Steve Lewer New Zealand Forest Owners Association Elizabeth Heeg Mackenzie District Council Karen Morgan, Mayor
descriptionSummary: Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill. Mount Manganui ratepayers residents and retailers. Tim Maltby

Now, article three of the treaty grants Maori the same rights and privileges as crown subjects, essentially citizenship. Now the opposite of that is that all New Zealanders should have the same rights and privileges as Maori. There's no grant of extra privileges to Maori and the treaty.

So all New Zealand citizens, including Maori, should have equal rights under the law. All New Zealanders should be treated equally. So governance clearly here is defined as the responsibility to make decisions.

So there's no need to create extra processes that are exclusive to Maori. There should be no special treatment for Maori that is not extended equally to all New Zealanders and TGI knowledge shouldn't have any special place in New Zealand legislation either. All cultural beliefs should be equal and have the same status.

So I think I'm asking that all ma specific clauses should either be removed or applied equally to all New Zealanders. So I'm using example Schedule 11 rates relief on Maori land. Now look, as a rate payer, all New Zealanders would love rates relief.

So granting rates relief to one group pushes an extra burden onto other rate payers. So there should be no special treatment of Maori or any other group, um, regarding rates. So I'm asking that rates relief Maori should either be repealed or it should apply equally to all New Zealanders.

So Maori should be contributing to rates and taxes. Equally with all other New Zealanders. Maori business makes up about 20% of the New Zealand economy, and Maori should not be a special class under the law. Now these are some specific clauses from Schedule 11.

So Schedule 11 should either be applied equally to everybody in New Zealand or it should be repealed. And if it is applied, it should be government funded, not rate payer funded. If you just push it onto rate payers, what you're doing is letting one group of rate payers off and putting an extra burden on other rate payers.

Uh, welcome Mar, Have 10 minutes. Can everybody hear me? Yes. 10 Minutes. Great. Okay. I was that my, um, my mic was gonna not work. Okay. Koto, um, good morning committee members. My name is Marni Prickett. I'm coordinator of auditor, the, the Al New Zealand Climate and Health Council.

And with me is Dr. Summer Wright, who is our convener and our executive, um, board member. AYA is an incorporated society. It represents over 1000 health professionals and organizations working on rapid, equitable, and regenerative responses to climate change. Our members are doctors, they are nurses, they are other medical staff and healthcare workers, as well as health researchers, policy makers, and other health organizations.

Um, and collectively. Collectively, we are urging our political leaders, including, um, the select committee, uh, to recognize that climate change and health can and should be addressed together to achieve the greatest benefit to our communities and society. Climate change is recognized internationally as one of the greatest threats to human health through indirect and direct impacts.

And auditor has a strong interest in this bill due to its implications for regional community-based climate, regional and community-based climate change responses. And because of its implications for people's health, uh, local government plays a key role in climate change mitigation, adaptation, resilience, building by consulting on developing and implementing land use, transport and other systems at regional catchment and community level.

Local government acts as a connector, a really a central connector between national policy and regional and community level action. And local government at its best can establish enduring responses by working with residents, iwi, hapu, local organizations and businesses, health agencies, and others to ensure that climate change responses are technically, socially and culturally sound and equitable.

Well-designed local climate change planning can protect and even improve communities' health. It can lower inequities and it can reduce our long-term costs. These functions of consultation, long-term planning and implementation are vital to communities, to our communities and countries present and future.

And we have all seen now where poor planning and a lack of consideration about a community's broader health environment and economic needs have led to climate change, amplified storm events, driving walls of mud and forest debris down valleys and into people's home and homes and businesses, and destroying critical public infrastructure like drinking water, wastewater treatment plants, et cetera.

And that's just one example of where poor planning has serious financial and human costs to communities. This bill, however, would restrict it, would have the effect of restricting local government's abilities to do the necessary broad consultation, research and planning to reduce the impact of climate change on communities.

The proposed core functions include civil defense emergency management, but no role in efforts to reduce or mitigate or prevent the emergencies through good planning. The bill emphasizes council's responsibility for responding to emergencies, but restricts their ability to plan for and implement measures that would reduce the severity of the impacts of natural hazards.

By doing this, the government is demanding an ambulance is parked at the bottom of the cliff while taking away the safety barriers, the safe paths, and the signs at the top of the cliff that would prevent people from falling in the first place. Restricting council's, consultation, planning, and implementation functions at the very least, uh, or at the very least I should say, rendering council's utterly confused by poorly conceived legislation like this that contradicts other legislative requirements as irresponsible.

Not least of all, because such confusion will do nothing to reduce council costs as this amendment is intended to do. Good governance and fiscal responsibility is unlikely when you unleash confusion through such an obviously rushed and poorly considered bill like this. In fact, without well evidenced equitable, socially and culturally sound, regional planning and implementation, the cost to communities will inevitably increase with climate change leading to work more and worse, infrastructure damage to be repaired, homes,

businesses, and lives lost, communities cut off. And the buffering effects that we rely on from healthy inco ecosystems reduced or lost. Additionally, this government is failing the country to produce enduring legislation by removing ity from public policy. We see that the single most consistent thread throughout the government's decisions over the last two years has been to take every opportunity to remove Maori rights.

And that has consequences for all of us. For all New Zealanders, Ang is, is fundamental to New Zealand's constitutional foundation. It's fundamental to equity and equity equitable responses, and it's fundamental to good governance. In this bill, the removal of the requirement for councils to consider Tik Maori knowledge when appointing council controlled organization directors erodes council's cultural competence and it will weaken the ability of councils to support communities to develop enduring plans for their resilience and their health.

In the face of climate change, individuals cannot protect their health, their homes, and their livelihoods alone in the face of climate change, local governance and collective action is a necessity. However, this bill intends to radically restrict council's roles in doing this without offering thought to how else local adaptation, mitigation, and resilience, uh, measures will be consulted on, developed or implemented.

Who will do this work if local councils don't? This bill appears rushed, it appears poorly conceived, but it has major consequences for all New Zealanders. So finally, we recommend that the bill is rejected by select committee, but, but we also recommend where there are reforms needed for local government, that there is a much more detailed and broadly consulted public process carried out, which includes a non-partisan agreement on a problem definition for local government.

And that through that we refine the role of local government rather than ad hoc changes such are in are in this bill. We've seen rates go up very, very fast, particularly in the last three years, um, you know, faster than any time that I was on local government. Um, I might say. Um, but how do we do that in a way which you think would work? Because obviously a lot of people who are paying those rates are really struggling with them. Yeah. and and rate payers. If you can't simply stop spending and hope that climate change and its impacts will stop, um, that's just unrealistic. Climate change is here and it's already having an impact. It's going to radically impact, uh, rate payers if we don't do take preventative measures.

Yeah, I've got that. So And so you, so you want to stop you, you wanna stop rates increasing, but you don't wanna take any preventative measures around.

And so those things don't go together. So you can't just, what we're saying is this is ad hoc. Chair. We have, uh, the next two submitters up here, but thanks District council be 12 five, ready to go. You hang on. Is here. What's that? Recreation hotel. Oh, okay. Sarah's here. I'm just gonna grab some water. There is water. So there.

Welcome. Welcome, Sarah. Thank you. We didn't realize you were actually there. I did. That's good. You should have said 10 minutes for your submission. Thank you so much for joining us. Uh, good morning Madam Chair and members of the select committee. Uh, my name is Sarah Murray and I'm the Chief Executive of Recreational Tua.

I appear before you today on behalf of recreational Tua, the professional association and industry body re representing the collective voice of recreation in New Zealand. Our mission is enhancing wellbeing through recreation and we work across New Zealand supporting our members, many of whom work for local government. From large metropolitan councils, managing extensive park and community facility networks through to small rural authorities struggling to maintain a single aquatic facility.

We welcome the opportunities to submit on the local government system improvements amendment bill. We acknowledge the government's intent to address rates, affordability and fiscal discipline. However, recreation Alterra is gravely concerned that several aspects of this bill risk undermining council's ability to deliver the essential outcomes communities need.

We are here today because local government is the single biggest investor in recreation, play, and sports infrastructure, spending over 1.3 billion annually on facilities and services crucial to community life. Our primary opposition lies in the proposal to remove the four aspects of wellbeing, social, cultural, economic, and environmental.

From the purpose of local government wellbeing we submit is not an optional extra, it's the foundation of community life. Recreation is not discretionary, it's not a nice to have, but a fundamental contributor to New Zealand's physical and mental health, social cohesion, youth development and climate resilience. Narrowing the purpose of local government to focus merely on cost effective service delivery as the bill proposes, will inevitably shift focus and funding away from crucial investments such as parks, libraries, cemeteries, recreation facilities, and the arts.

These investments are easy targets when councils face fiscal pressure, but their absence actively enro erodes the social and cultural fabric of our communities. Access to public open space alone, a critical local government function is consistently ranked as a key driver of the quality of life demonstrably reducing healthcare costs and increasing physical activity.

So we strongly urge the committee to retain the wellbeing purpose in the local government Act 2002, uh, we likewise oppose the exclusion of symmetries and crematoria from the bill. We acknowledge positively that the bill explicitly recognizes reserves, libraries, museums, and recreational facilities as core services.

This is an affirmation that recreation is indeed essential, not discretionary. However, we we're de deeply concerned by the inexplicable exclusion of cemeteries and crematoria from this core services list. This exclusion is inconsistent with the burial and cremation act 1964, which obliges territorial authorities to provide cemeteries.

Cemeteries and crematoria are also not optional amenities. They are essential services, they're required by law and they're also fundamental to community wellbeing within our organization. Recreation Alter, uh, sits the New Zealand cemeteries and Crematoria Collective Naro Tu a network representing professionals managing these spaces.

Um, and that network would tell you, and we tell you also that these are absolutely critical facilities. They provide dignity and death for families in whanau. They honor cultural and spiritual traditions, especially for ma Maori Pacifica and migrant communities. And they preserve community heritage in Whakapapa, excluding them creates legislative inconsistency, sends a damaging message that they are optional and diminishes their vital cultural and spiritual significance as places of remembrance and connection to ua.

This perpetuates a history of neglect and underinvestment investment in this sector. The New Zealand Crematory, cemeteries and Crematoria Collective and Recreation urge that cemeteries and crematoria be explicitly recognized as core local government services. Furthermore, we call on this government to take a strategic holistic approach to modernizing the outdated legislative framework governing burial, cremation and local government.

Without this recognition and national standards, communities risk, inequitable access to dignified burial cremation on the issue of financial sustainability and accountability. Uh, the recreation sector currently faces severe financial strains. Most councils are nearing debt ceilings, grappling with climate adaptation costs, and dealing with inflation and high energy costs, particularly those running aquatic facilities that rely on LPG heating.

To address these financial realities, we support the principle of increasing transparency through activity based reporting. To ensure this reporting is meaningful, we recommend the inclusion of recognized industry specific benchmarking tools such as yardstick for parks and recreation. Benchmarks should include metrics like accessibility.

For example, the percentage of residents within 500 meters of green space. And also metrics around facility condition for symmetries be benchmarks should cover capacity planning and affordability. Regarding funding mechanisms, the bill clarifies that third party contributions can offset de development contributions.

Recreation alter strongly recommends this provision be explicitly extended to cover operational expenditure such as energy staffing and MA maintenance explicitly allowing development contributions to offset both capital and operating costs would be a vital step towards unlocking more sustainable provision. We submit that the bill overlooks important systemic issues as well.

While we appreciate the focus on affordability, framing this bill narrowly as a response to rate payer costs fails to address the deeper enduring challenges faced by local government. The 2023 review into the future for local government called for five fundamental shifts strengthened democracy, a wellbeing focus, treaty partnerships, genuine central local partnership and equitable funding.

This bill, instead of building on these findings, opts to narrow the purpose of local governments centralized control and avoid funding reform. We believe this is a huge missed opportunity. We recommend that the bill be aligned with the future for local government review findings, particularly concerning funding reform.

Local government funding mechanisms need to be reformed, including exploring, sharing GST on rates, creating dedicated climate adaptation funds and requiring crown contributions to local mandates. We must always, we must also recognize climate adaptation is a core local government role, intrinsically linked to parks, recreation, and cemetery provision.

So in conclusion, recreational supports improvements and accountability and affordability. However, the local government system improvements amendment bill must not compromise the crucial elements that sustain our communities, namely, wellbeing, democracy, and essential services. We strongly urge the select committee to amend the bill to retain the wellbeing purpose in the local government.

Act 2002, recognize recreation, infrastructure and services as well as cemeteries and crematoria as essential services. And importantly, to address essential long-term issues relating to funding, benchmarking and democratic renewal. Recreation play in remembrance are vital to New Zealand's future.

They underpin health, cohesion, resilience, and dignity. Narrowing the role of local government undermines these critical outcomes. We ask the committee to empower councils to continue providing the spaces and services that keep communities active, healthy, resilient, and connected. Uh, thank you for your time and consideration and um, if there's time, I'm happy to take any questions.

Time for one quick question if all has one, Sarah, we've had a lot of those. Um, thanks for that. We've had a lot of those, um, sort of comments. More money wanted. You got that, but I'm not hearing also councils taking some responsibility for saying actually we could be more efficient and effective. And I know there's, and I've been through many budget processes, you know, but it seems like it's all somebody else's problem to fix.

Is this something that needs to be a bit of two way? Oh, absolutely. And um, and I hope that didn't come from the submission because that is not our suggestion now. Not necessarily yours, but others as well. Our, our our, so what we're asking for here and what suggesting is that the um, the significant and um, very useful piece of work that was undertaken and published last year around the future for local government is really, um, considered as, um, underpinning, um, this bill and the approach that, um, that this government chooses to take.

So, um, this is not about, uh, central government, uh, being solely responsible or, um, providing all of the funding for these essential services and councils. It's about having a, um, a better and more equitable, uh, split in system so that we set up councils to succeed. 'cause they are not set up to succeed at the moment.

They are set up, uh, to fail. And we need to change that. And you as a government have a responsibility, um, to support that through, um, the choices you make with this bill. Welcome. Welcome. Thank you. You've got 10 minutes. Okay. Thank you. Uh, Queenstown Lakes district is one of the fastest growing districts in the country. In New Zealand's most sought after premier visitor destination. At peak times, there are 2.25 visitors per resident in the district.

More than doubling the population of our district, due to the pressures of growth and tourism, our district has taken the approach to getting the basics right and its long term plan. The approach pri uh, prioritized essential infrastructure and maintaining a consistent level of service instead of taking on discretionary projects.

As shown in DIA's council profile QLDC allocated 89% of its 2024 capital expenditure towards water eroding related activities with much of the remainder going towards community facilities. This approach aligns strongly with the behaviors this bill looks to encourage.

But even after a council explicitly prioritize the basics, first rates rises when necessitated to ensure the district continues to function. This demonstrates that the lack of financial dis uh, fiscal discipline and broad purpose of local government is not the primary driver of rates rises, but rather increasing capital costs previous underinvestment in the inability of for local government to utilize a variety of funding and financing tools to ensure growth pays for growth.

This is why QLDC has been a strong supporter of the Regional deals program, but does not support the proposed change to local government laid out in this bill. Changing the purpose will not address the fundamental issues and distracts from more concrete and tangible solutions that are needed to address cost pressures for local government and New Zealand's freight paths.

Refocusing the purpose of local government councils are the closest form of government to the people. They have deep community ties that can give vocal, uh, give voice to local issues. And narrow focus of local government runs the risk of limiting council's ability to address issues raised through the democratic process and minimize the role councils play as an enabler of community and environmental development efforts.

Many, many councils create partnerships with the community as well as central government. And most of that work by the community is done on a volunteer basis. This bill puts that at risk. In response to those concerns, QLEC recommends a more holistic approach to the purpose of local government that acknowledges territorial author's role as a key partner in creating and delivering local solutions.

That the proposed purpose of local government, section 10 C, is amended to support sustainable local economic growth and development. And council supports the recognition of the needs of future communities when providing infrastructure and calls for greater recognition throughout the act. Such as in section 1 0 1 2 B.

The Legal risk Council also recommends that the definition of cost effective in the proposed purpose of the local government, um, for local government is altered. This is to acknowledge council considers a variety of criteria when making decisions such as environmental, community, social equity, and intergenerational considerations.

Having a sole requirement on the focus of cost effectiveness effectiveness runs the risk of opening council to legal risk and challenge by well sourced parties looking to influence decision making processes. Legislative certainty, responding to and contributing towards reforms and legislative changes requires resource and disrupts long-term planning process.

Like any service provider councils require legislative certainty to focus on increasing efficiencies and to plan QLEC requests that the government more explicitly consider the administration and and disruption costs of the sector when carrying out reforms. Government can provide more certainty to councils through this process by explicitly stating the transition period for the amended purpose of local government and for consideration of council timelines such as long-term plan and annual planning when setting a transition period.

Other points clarification. The standardized code of conduct conduct is complemented with, uh, is complemented with training and development for opportunities for elected members. Changes to broad standing orders model can be changed through a 75% threshold to ensure councils can adapt the model to local context if required.

My name is Liz Davies and I'm the Chief Executive of Social Link to hoori, the umbrella or the peak organization supporting the social community sector in the Western Bay of Plenty.

Uh, we represent over 1500 organizations and individuals working every day to strengthen the wellbeing of communities. So I'm here today to express social link strong opposition to the proposed changes to section 10 of the bill, which would remove the four pillars of community wellbeing from the local government Act 2002, uh, local government was established to meet the needs of the communities that they serve.

These needs are broad brush and summed up in the four pillars of wellbeing. Social, economic, environmental, and cultural. Local government has never ever been just about roads rates in rubbish. Local councils are expected by communities and by legislation to provide and support a wealth of services and facilities from urban regeneration, local economic development, environment protection, local roads, sports fields, and community hubs, events, and many more.

All of these come under the four wellbeings. They, they're four pillars providing a platform for livable, vibrant, productive, flourishing communities. Removing these pillars from legislation may end up limiting local government's ability to meet these and other needs. Councils have robust consultation processes that ensure communities' voices are heard when setting plans, priorities, and budgets.

And local communities want more control over their destinies, not less. If the four wellbeings are removed, council may no longer have the legislative backing to respond to what communities actually want. In addition, if we strip away the wellbeings, we risk turning councils into service contractors focused on cost alone, not on creating places where people belong, thrive, and participate.

And we're also concerned about the bill's proposed new focus on local economic growth and development. While economic prosperity is important, it cannot be the sole measure of success. A strong economy doesn't automatically mean a strong community. Have we focused on cost efficiency and economic return. We risk neglecting the social connections, cultural heritage, and environmental stewardship that makes communities truly resilient.

We acknowledge the government's concerns about rising rates and fiscal discipline. Many rate payers, especially those on fixed incomes, as struggling with cost of living pressures. However, removing the four wellbeings will not address this issue. Most rate increases are driven by ongoing and increasing infrastructure needs and past underinvestment, not by so-called nice to haves.

And one person's nice to haves is another's bottom line of necessary infrastructure to create a flourishing community where everyone belongs. The nice to have term is very subjective and not helpful in determining what councils should or should not do. The majority of local government spending already focuses on core services.

The real problem is the limited revenue tools available to councils. The government wants to improve fiscal sustainability. It should look at expanding funding and financing options, not narrowing the purpose of local government. I recently went to the opening of the, um, Maryvale Community Center and one of to long's most deprived communities.

It was a long time coming, but this center is going to be a game changer for the community. It will deliver on all four wellbeings, sustain the community with Kai, offer training, mentoring and guidance to nurture young people into employment, education and training. Provide a space for community connect connection, help rebuild people's lives with counseling and care and connect people to their culture.

This bill may have resulted in this center not being built, which would've been such a significant loss on so many different levels for this community. In conclusion, the removal of the four wheel beings would send the wrong signal to councils that community, environment, culture, and social cohesion no longer matter as much as cost efficiency.

We believe all four wellbeings must remain in the purpose of local government. They're the compass that ensures local government decisions are grounded in the values and aspirations of the people they serve. But thank you very much. Questions. I'll ask one then.
